words: ROB MOORE: I wanted to ask you again about moonshining. How would you go and
set yourself up? How would you build a still?
ULYSSES SHOWALTER: Well, you're going to put that on tape?

speaker: ROB
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: US
words: I don't know, should we?

speaker: ROB
words: Oh sure. There's nothing wrong with that is there?

speaker: US
words: No, there ain't nothing wrong with it. I'd make some more right now if I

speaker: US
words: dared (laughs) because the moonshine that I made was medicine. You know,

speaker: US
words: you'd never have any stomach trouble if you had some of that every day.

speaker: ROB
words: Why is that?

speaker: US
words: Well, it was the stuff it was made out of. It was made out of bran instead

speaker: US
words: of other grain, you see. Bran has a tendency, you know, to move your bowels,

speaker: US
words: that bran. It's the juice out of it. I'll tell you anyway. I don't suppose

speaker: US
words: you can get it now, you might get it somewhere, but now wait though. If

speaker: US
words: you get these here copper boilers, wash boilers, you know what I mean don't

speaker: US
words: ya? (Yes.) You make one right out of that. Or you can have it made by

speaker: US
words: some tinsmith, you know.

speaker: ROB
words: Okay. You have a boiler and what do you do with that?

speaker: US
words: Well you see if you got the boiler (but of course you can have it made,, you

speaker: US
words: see, this other stuff too might be a little handier than the wash boiler) but

speaker: US
words: you see you take the lid and you cut a hole in about the middle of the top

speaker: US
words: of the lid. Then you have (of course I could see to solder) and I soldered

speaker: US
words: me a funnel to set on that, then run over here and run out.

speaker: ROB
words: Is that where you use the copper tubing?

speaker: US
words: Yeah, then it runs into the copper tubing, you see.

speaker: ROB
words: Oh, from the top of the boiler.

speaker: US
words: From the top of the boiler, yes. Fits in there and it runs on through on

speaker: US
words: that. You'd have a wooden keg, or any kind, metal or anything, for water.

speaker: US
words: That would hold water, have water running in there to keep it cool, you see,

speaker: US
words: that's what...

speaker: ROB
words: And the coil runs through that?

speaker: US
words: Yeah, and the coil runs through the water. And bourse the water's running

speaker: US
words: and you got to keep that cool if it gets too hot and it evaporates it won't

speaker: US
words: make the moonshine. See what it does is the steam it makes runs through

speaker: US
words: that water, condenses and runs down into your vessel, moonshine. It's strong

speaker: US
words: at first. Probably well...

speaker: ROB
words: What is double-run moonshine?

speaker: US
words: Well double-run means, say put your mash in a barrel, you know, wooden barrel.

speaker: US
words: You're taking this all down now? (Yeah.) See you should take your 50 gallon

speaker: US
words: barrel, wooden barrel, you can take a smaller one as far as that's concerned.

speaker: US
words: But anyway you should take it and pour gasoline in it and set it afire, you

speaker: US
words: know what I mean? The barrel, char it a little all around. 'Course you'd

speaker: US
words: wanta look out or you'll burn a hole in it, too. But that keeps it from tast

speaker: US
words: ing like wood. If you put it in a barrel long enough without having it charred,

speaker: US
words: it gets to tasting woody. Kind of sour like and something. But then this here,

speaker: US
words: you char it and then put it in. Of course, I could tell you all about it.

speaker: US
words: I built a little shack out of logs I guess it was about eight feet by six or

speaker: US
words: maybe eight by eight, I don't remember. But you got an idea anyhow. A little

speaker: US
words: shack out of logs. It had corners in there, two by fours, nailed 'em together.

speaker: US
words: Corners, you know. 'Course you could build that little log shack any way you

speaker: US
words: wanted to. That was the easiest way. Cut your logs just the right length

speaker: US
words: and you can put 'em in there and nail in these two by fours, you know, on the

speaker: US
words: end. But that's immaterial because you could make it out of lumber anything,

speaker: US
words: a little shack, or of course you can get out without any shack. Take oatmeal

speaker: US
words: and make your moonshine. Say in the summertin^ if you was outside then you'd

speaker: US
words: have your little bench, your barrel sittin' on it. Rut a lantern just under

speaker: US
words: this bench you see. Well, now wait. There's a little thing you know, you'd

speaker: US
words: built up and set the barrel on top. But it had a place under there to stick

speaker: US
words: the lantern under you know, and turn it down. If you turn it on full blast

speaker: US
words: it'd get too hot. Your mash would, you'd lose it.

speaker: ROB
words: That's before you put it in the still?

speaker: US
words: Yeah. And then when it sours so long...

speaker: ROB
words: How long is it suppose to sour?

speaker: US
words: Well, til it quits workin*. And the way to do it, now I can tell ya 50

speaker: US
words: pounds of sugar to a 50 gallon barrel. And about three gallon of bran. And

speaker: US
words: then the yeast to make it work. But of course you can put corn in there too,

speaker: US
words: and give it the corn flavor.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you usually do that?

speaker: US
words: After.I got wise to it I did. See, 'course I started milking what one fella

speaker: US
words: told me and worked up my different one you know. A double-run, see, is say

speaker: US
words: for instance here you take it out of the barrel. You strain that through a

speaker: US
words: gunny sack or anything, you know, into your still.

speaker: ROB
words: Oh, so you take all the solid stuff out?

speaker: US
words: Yeah. And then you run it through first til it gets down til just about all

speaker: US
words: the alcohol out of the there. When it gets coming, let's say wou'd have a

speaker: US
words: little tester, see they used to (I don't know if you can get 'em now or not,

speaker: US
words: but that was durhing the Prohibition) you could get testers at any drugstore.

speaker: US
words: Test the hell out of it.

speaker: ROB
words: Why is that? Why do you think you could do that? If you went and bought

speaker: ROB
words: a tester wouldn't people know you were going to buy it to make moonshine with.

speaker: US
words: Yah, but who cared them days. Everybody was making moonshine in those days,

speaker: US
words: (laughs). Just about. After I got caught for moonshining why I found out all

speaker: US
words: my neighbors was making it too. (Oh really.) Yeah, that was practically all

speaker: US
words: of 'em. Gust two or three of us got caught around then. 'Course I could go

speaker: US
words: in and make a long story out of it but I ain't goin' to, about that. But

speaker: US
words: anyway you run it through, you see, til you pret'near get all the alcohol out

speaker: US
words: of it and it probably would test 70 per cent then. That's the first run.

speaker: US
words: Well, maybe that ain't right, but it's close anyway. Then you put it aside,

speaker: US
words: put it in a vessel to hold it. I had a big 20 gallon crock you know, jar,

speaker: US
words: that I put that in, then I'd run the other barrel, if I had two barrels set

speaker: US
words: at once. Well, for now I'll just go from the one instead of taking the two.

speaker: US
words: (Okay.) Take this here, and reduce it with water, plain water, to 60 per

speaker: US
words: cent.

speaker: ROB
words: 60 per cent or 60 proof?

speaker: US
words: 60 proof. (Okay.) If you get it much stronger than that then you're liable

speaker: US
words: to blow your still up. Then you can run it again. Put it in there, 60 proof,

speaker: US
words: and run it again. Heat, you got heat. I had a little oil stove that I had

speaker: US
words: on it, used to run mine with. 'Course you could run it on a cookstove or

speaker: US
words: anything like the oldtimers I don't know, you have trouble runnin' it on an

speaker: US
words: electric cook stove (laughs). Now gas, you can run it on one of these gas

speaker: US
words: stoves, too. Heat it you see. Not run it too fast anytime but just about so.

speaker: US
words: Then you get it down to...well the way I done it was 100, you see, after I

speaker: US
words: was double-runnin* it back to a 100, got so's I could catch some of that in

speaker: US
words: that little tube, you know, and test it to 100. Then I'd cut that off and

speaker: US
words: run the.rest of that. I'd cut that off there, it would be 140 and then I'd

speaker: US
words: reduce it down to 100. I had two testers, one was a government tester and

speaker: US
words: the other was the little tube tester. 'Course it wasn't government, out you

speaker: US
words: could tell what you was doing by it.

speaker: US
words: I was making some down there at Dayton, and there was a fella was hellering

speaker: US
words: about it wasn't strong enough. I'd tested it in the cold, cold weather, and

speaker: US
words: it would test at 100 in that tube tester. It didn't burn, you know, like roost

speaker: US
words: strong whiskey. And he thought it was too weak. I didn't dare tell him that

speaker: US
words: I was makin' it. But I told him I seen the fella test it. The way I tested

speaker: US
words: it, you see, was down in the basement and it was cold. I tested it and it

speaker: US
words: was up to 100 then with that tube and of course that had it normal like it

speaker: US
words: come from the still, like it supposed to be. But it tested at about 118. And

speaker: US
words: he didn't think it was stiff enough. But of course I learned a lot of that

speaker: US
words: after I was talking to him. Of course as you went along you learnt more, you

speaker: US
words: know, learnt more. It was just wonderful. And then of course to color it,

speaker: US
words: well I'd reduce in here and cut it off at a 100, you know. Tested a 100i

speaker: ROB
words: What would you thin it out with?

speaker: US
words: With good clear spring water, you know. 'Course I had all the spring water

speaker: US
words: in the world there where I was runnin' it. I tested it down with that to the

speaker: US
words: 100. Test 100 with that there. That double-run would test 140, would test

speaker: US
words: 140 proof. Then I'd reduce it down to that 100 with spring water. 'Course

speaker: US
words: I had the government tester there too. And that showed you a lot different

speaker: US
words: than that other one. It didn't matter whether your liquor was warm or cold.

speaker: US
words: It'd give you the right test to it, the government tester did. 'Course I

speaker: US
words: don't know where a feller would ever get a government tester now.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you say that you used to color it also?

speaker: US
words: Yeah, then we'd color it. 'Course my brother was up at Spokane, and I

speaker: US
words: found out the thing to do it to color it, to make it taste like old whiskey.

speaker: US
words: What made the old color and everything was the keg that it was put in, you

speaker: US
words: see. And time. See, you put it in a barrel, they're all charred I think, put

speaker: US
words: it in there and leave it a year. See, this was beating the time by taking the

speaker: US
words: oak shavings and scorching 'em and put 'em in. Well, say you had that big

speaker: US
words: jar. Twenty gallons, probably fifteen gallon, and put in about balf a gallon

speaker: US
words: of them shavings, them parched oak shavings. You can buy 'em by the gunny

speaker: US
words: sack full in Spokane. My brother, he sent up there for 'em to get one and

speaker: US
words: send it down. A sack you know.

speaker: ROB
words: Was your brother a moonshiner too?

speaker: US
words: He knew how. He wasn't moonshining. He made some once in awhile for his

speaker: US
words: own use but he didn't try to make a business out of it. See, the reason I

speaker: US
words: was making a business out of it was heck! you couldn't get any work. Started

speaker: US
words: in in '29 I think, or something like that when I started making it, but then...

speaker: US
words: let's see now I don't remember just what time it was. But anyway, I'd sell

speaker: US
words: it for five dollars a gallon. 'Course there was other fellers...that was the

speaker: US
words: reason I got caught. There was other fellers that was selling their booze for

speaker: US
words: four dollars, you see, and they'd buy mine for five and in preference to this

speaker: US
words: other for four.

speaker: ROB
words: Were there a lot of people that was making moonshine to sell?

speaker: US
words: Yeah. Gosh, there wasn't no work. If you cut wood you might get a few

speaker: US
words: cord to cut, and if you worked in the sawmill maybe you'd get two days a week

speaker: US
words: at two dollars a day if you could get it. But you couldn't hardly get work.

speaker: US
words: just a few, you know.

speaker: ROB
words: How much money could you make off of one batch of moon?

speaker: US
words: Well, I got so I could run about six gallon out of one barrel. That would

speaker: US
words: be 30 dollars. Then you had to buy your sugar, and your bran, and your yeast,

speaker: US
words: and the jugs. There's one thing and another. I'd been doing pretty well then,

speaker: US
words: you know, for just a common plug for a living as a man. Living and paying

speaker: US
words: a few debts, you see.

speaker: ROB
words: How long would it take you to make that batch?

speaker: US
words: Well I'll tell you. After you get started you could get a barrel of mash

speaker: US
words: ready to go in about four days or less sometimes. It depended a little on

speaker: US
words: circumstances. Four days, then you can run that off. You just take one

speaker: US
words: barrel, you'd run that off and double it back in about one day. 'Course you

speaker: US
words: see night nor morning nor day didn't make any difference to me. I could go

speaker: US
words: to bed and set the clock, the alarm, to go off so's I could go to sleep and

speaker: US
words: get up and go and attend to it different times like that. 'Course if you

speaker: US
words: want a real start to make it, why you can come back again we can write it all

speaker: US
words: down. I have it here but I'm jumping around so darn much with my thought I'm

speaker: US
words: going to mix you up.

speaker: ROB
words: Was there a lot of competition between different moonshiners?

speaker: US
words: Not a awful lot. There's just a few crooked moonshiners. Like this one

speaker: US
words: fella, he tricked me. He didn't make it at that. One fella, you know, he

speaker: US
words: got jealous. His moonshine...he had trouble selling it for four dollars and

speaker: US
words: they'd buy mine right out from under him for five dollars. So he figured a

speaker: US
words: scheme. Had his boy to get a friend...there was one feller there, a Scotsman,

speaker: US
words: him and I didn't get along at all, the Scotsman and I. He had a boy about

speaker: US
words: seventeen or eighteen. So this other feller, this moonshiner boy...'dourse

speaker: US
words: the old man himself was the father of the deal. See, they got him...he was

speaker: US
words: a snooper. He'd look around there and he told me one day says, "Everytime,"

speaker: US
words: he says, "you move your still," (I'd move it in the summertime, it wouldn't

speaker: US
words: take long to move the darn thing) he says, "Every time you moved your still,"

speaker: US
words: he says, "I knew where it was at." So he had to snoop to find it, he had

speaker: US
words: to snoop around to see where the still was. So then he got his boy, his oldest

speaker: US
words: boy, to take this seventeen or eighteen year old boy, and told him where

speaker: US
words: my still was and they run right down on to it. Of course he knew where it

speaker: US
words: was but...

speaker: ROB
words: Now who was this that ran right into it?

speaker: US
words: It was the other moonshiner's boy and then .this here Scotsman's boy. He

speaker: US
words: figured if the Scotsman got ahold of it he'd peddle it all over the world.

speaker: US
words: And of course I guess he didn't know...but that wasn't where I got caught.

speaker: ROB
words: What did they do when they got there? Did they take your still or take the

speaker: ROB
words: moon or what?

speaker: US
words: They poured the moon out, all but some of it. They took a gallon of the

speaker: US
words: clear 140 proof stuff. And then I had about a little over a half gallon

speaker: US
words: already fixed up to drink. They took that. Well you see there's nothing honest

speaker: US
words: about that darn thing anyway. You see up there at Kellogg, it wasn't exactly

speaker: US
words: right at Kellogg, up there there was a creek and they called it Moon Creek.

speaker: US
words: The reason they called it Moon Creek was 'cause there was so much moonshine

speaker: US
words: made on it. There was a Italian, well maybe three or four of 'em I don't

speaker: US
words: know. Anyway they built a house there that would hold probably 100 of them

speaker: US
words: barrels. And they had it up in there. You didn't have to have a lantern

speaker: US
words: under it if you had it warm enough to let it work. So they had that warm

speaker: US
words: enough to work and they had the these here federals...you see they was protected

speaker: US
words: by these Federals they figured, clear from the moon Moon Creek in there clear

speaker: US
words: into Spokane.

speaker: ROB
words: How were they protected?

speaker: US
words: Well they...what do you call them darn things? They're moonshine hunters

speaker: US
words: though.

speaker: ROB
words: Revenuers.

speaker: US
words: Revenuers. They're the guys and they protect 'em. Well now let's say I

speaker: US
words: wanted. at that time we all knew about it and wanted too. Of course at my

speaker: US
words: size why I couldn't afford to pay protection for the amount of moon I was

speaker: US
words: making. 'Cause they paid 35 dollars a day for protection, you see.

speaker: ROB
words: That was up at Moon Creek they did that?

speaker: US
words: Yeah. At one time. See after they used it and left it...they left all

speaker: US
words: their barrels there. See they run...I don't know I'm getting mixed up. But

speaker: US
words: it wouldn't matter anyway. Seems like it was 3,000 gallons or somethin* of

speaker: US
words: moon they run there. That one outfit, see? They were protected about 35

speaker: US
words: days after they got started. But anyway if you wanted protection, you'd

speaker: US
words: know who these revenuers was. Well you could go to one of 'em and tell 'em

speaker: US
words: you'd like some protection. "Oh no, you're looking up the wrong feller don't fool

speaker: US
words: around me or I'll

speaker: US
words: have you put in jail." And he says, "If you don't believe it you go over here

speaker: US
words: and see Bob Moon. He'll tell ya." So you go over and see Bob Moon and he'd say, "How

speaker: US
words: much moon this time

speaker: US
words: do you want to make?" You tell him and he'll tell ya how much you had to pay

speaker: US
words: to make it. So...

speaker: ROB
words: Oh I see. So they wouldn't be directly involved with it?

speaker: US
words: No, you couldn't prove anything with them Federals.

speaker: ROB
words: Did that happen around here too?

speaker: US
words: That happened, yeah. I don't know just where it was around here, but

speaker: US
words: I know'd about that one up at Moon because they was a fella that married my

speaker: US
words: niece that lived right there on Moon Creek. He wasn't the one that was making...

speaker: US
words: called them dago's. But you know what they was was off-breeds, those kinds

speaker: US
words: that was in there. It seemed like they cuuld get into things like that and

speaker: US
words: get out of it. But even at that, they didn't that one but a lot of times

speaker: US
words: fcha revenuers would double-cross 'em and catch 'em anyway. Have an outsider

speaker: US
words: come in and get 'em. They was framed both ways, you see.

speaker: ROB
words: Who were the revenuers here in Moscow?

speaker: US
words: Well there was no revenuers, really, but there was a feller here was a

speaker: US
words: federal man. But he told me he says, "I donH look for moon shiners," he says.

speaker: US
words: "They sign a warrant for me and I take 'em in."

speaker: ROB
words: Do you remember his name?

speaker: US
words: Yeah, Bob West. He owned this whole hill here once. You know all the

speaker: US
words: houses on it. A lot of the land too.

speaker: ROB
words: Was he the man who arrested you?

speaker: US
words: No. The ones that arrested me was from up at Coeur d'Alene. No, up from

speaker: US
words: Kellogg one of them was and the other one belonged to Coeur d'Alene. Let's

speaker: US
words: see I forget their names.

speaker: ROB
words: Were they accompanied by a local sheriff too?

speaker: US
words: Yes, they was accompanied by the sheriff here.

speaker: ROB
words: Which one was that?

speaker: US
words: Sheriff Summerfield. He was a good guy too.

speaker: ROB
words: How was it that you came to be arrested? How did that happen?

speaker: US
words: Well, there was a fella was runnin' a restaurant down here on Third Street

speaker: US
words: and selling moonshine on the side. And I never seen the fella...

speaker: ROB
words: Who was that?

speaker: US
words: I don't know if there's some of his relations around here yet. I won't tell

speaker: US
words: ya. (Okay.) But anyway...

speaker: ROB
words: I don't think it makes any difference at this point...

speaker: US
words: I'm talking about some of his folks might not ever knowed about him. About

speaker: US
words: being into moonshining or something...

speaker: ROB
words: They'd probably be proud of it by now, you know.

speaker: US
words: They might be. But the chances are...they're a lot of people thats queer,

speaker: US
words: See now, like here a lot of people in my shape that had made it and got caught

speaker: US
words: and put the search on. They wouldn't tell on a bet, you know, that they'd

speaker: US
words: ever been in jail. But me...it happened, what's the use in lieing about

speaker: US
words: it? (Right, exactly.) But anyway, I'd like to have some of that old moonshine

speaker: US
words: at about five dollars a gallon. For my stomeoh, you see. First started there

speaker: US
words: and I started making it. Well there was some people down in Kahlotus that

speaker: US
words: made it first. That's down this side of Pasco, probably fifty miles or more.

speaker: US
words: Yeah, I guess a little more than fifty miles above Pasco in the Dusty country.

speaker: US
words: Well anyway they had it down there. They'd run off fifteen or twenty gallons

speaker: US
words: before harvest so we had moonshine all harvest. You know take a drink in

speaker: US
words: the morning, drink at noon, drink at night before supper. But anyway I had

speaker: US
words: ulcers, you see, and had be alert to that. It was up here (pointing to his

speaker: US
words: upper stomach). I started to making some. The work went out anyway, you know.

speaker: US
words: The logging camps at Potlatch, all of them, just about everyone of them,

speaker: US
words: shut down at that time. And so I went to making a little of that moonshine.

speaker: US
words: I had ulcers, you know what they are. When they get in your stomach it doesn't

speaker: US
words: feel good. But you know that darn moonshine cured that up. Of course then

speaker: US
words: I went from here...I had a little battle with a fella and he knew about me

speaker: US
words: making this moonshine and I thought I'd better get out. So I went up to

speaker: US
words: Sacheen Lake. I was up there and a friend that I used to know was a doing a

speaker: US
words: little bit of wood business, hauling wood to Spokane. Hauling about forty

speaker: US
words: miles. I guess it was a good forty miles from Sacheen Lake to Spokane. So

speaker: US
words: I got a job cutting a little wood. But it snowed so deep that you couldn't

speaker: US
words: work so I went down and borrowed some money from a fella to live on, baching.

speaker: US
words: It didn't take too much to bach. And later on that winter, killed deer for

speaker: US
words: meat. You know, you'd kill deer in there anytime, pert'near. As far as the

speaker: US
words: law is concerned they didn't pay much attention. That was during that hard

speaker: US
words: time, you see.

speaker: US
words: One old fella on the other side of the lake the next year he got caught

speaker: US
words: for moonshining. But he asked the game warden, he said, "What would you do?"

speaker: US
words: That was the year I was in there. He asked him, he said, "What would you do

speaker: US
words: if you caught me with a deer out of season?" 'Course it was out of season

speaker: US
words: in the wintertime. Well he says, "I'd arrest ya." (What did he say?) But

speaker: US
words: anyway he was saying there was nothing to it. By the time he got done, "It

speaker: US
words: was nothing," he said. That was the second winter. Why I had a little rig.

speaker: US
words: It was a six gallon thing that the Monkey Ward's and I guess Sears and Roebuck

speaker: US
words: all of 'em was making in those days especially for little moonshiners.

speaker: ROB
words: Oh, you could buy 'era through the catalogue?

speaker: US
words: Yeah. The only difference was you had to work 'em over a little bit. It

speaker: US
words: was six gallons, you see, with a slip-on lid. Then you put a rag around there
